question_html:
1. The wavelength of the electromagnetic wave is often correlated with the characteristic size of the system that radiates. Give two examples to justify this statement.
    1. Long distance radio broadcasts use short-wave bands. Why?
    2. Optical and radio telescopes are built on the ground, but X-ray astronomy is possible only from satellites orbiting the Earth. Why?
question_text: The wavelength of the electromagnetic wave is often correlated with the characteristic size of the system that radiates. Give two examples to justify this statement. Long distance radio broadcasts use short-wave bands. Why? Optical and radio telescopes are built on the ground, but X-ray astronomy is possible only from satellites orbiting the Earth. Why?
explanation: Radio Antennas: Long radio antennas are sized to match the wavelength of the broadcast signals they handle, enhancing signal efficiency and reception. Optical Telescopes: Large mirrors in optical telescopes match the wavelength of visible light to improve resolution and light-gathering capability. Short-Wave Bands: Short-wave radio signals (3 to 30 MHz) are used for long-distance broadcasts because they can reflect off the ionosphere, a layer of the Earth's atmosphere. This reflection allows them to travel beyond the horizon and cover vast distances, making them suitable for international broadcasts. Long-wave signals, on the other hand, tend to follow the curvature of the Earth and do not reflect as effectively, making them less suitable for long-distance communication. Optical and Radio Telescopes: These telescopes are built on the ground because visible light and radio waves can pass through the Earth's atmosphere. Optical telescopes collect visible light, while radio telescopes detect radio waves. The atmosphere does not significantly obstruct these wavelengths, so ground-based observatories can be very effective. X-Ray Astronomy: X-rays do not penetrate the Earth's atmosphere; they are absorbed by it. Therefore, X-ray telescopes must be placed in space to avoid atmospheric absorption. Satellites orbiting Earth provide the necessary environment to observe cosmic X-rays directly. This is why X-ray astronomy is conducted from space-based observatories rather than ground-based facilities.

  1. Obtain the value of the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave.

question_text: The oscillating electric field of an electromagnetic wave is given by: {tex}{E_y} = 30\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]V{m^{ - 1}}{/tex} Obtain the value of the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave. Write down the expression for the oscillating magnetic field.
explanation: {tex}{E_y} = 30\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]V{m^{ - 1}}{/tex} On comparing the given equation with E y = E ₀ sin(ωt + kx), we get {tex}k = 300\pi = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{{150}} = 6.7 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} The wave is propagating along X-axis, electric field is oscillating along Y-axis, so according to right hand system of (vector E, vector B, vector K), the magnetic field must oscillate along Z-axis. On comparing the given equation with E = E ₀ sin(ωt + kx), we get, E o = 30 V/m Now, B o = {tex}\frac {E_o}c{/tex} = {tex}\frac{30}{3\times10^8}{/tex} = 10 -7 T Thus, equation of oscillating magnetic field is B z = B o sin(ωt + kx) {tex}\Rightarrow{B_z} = ({10^{ - 7}})\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]T{/tex}
solution: {tex}{E_y} = 30\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]V{m^{ - 1}}{/tex} On comparing the given equation with E y = E ₀ sin(ωt + kx), we get {tex}k = 300\pi = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{{150}} = 6.7 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex} The wave is propagating along X-axis, electric field is oscillating along Y-axis, so according to right hand system of (vector E, vector B, vector K), the magnetic field must oscillate along Z-axis. On comparing the given equation with E = E ₀ sin(ωt + kx), we get, E o = 30 V/m Now, B o = {tex}\frac {E_o}c{/tex} = {tex}\frac{30}{3\times10^8}{/tex} = 10 -7 T Thus, equation of oscillating magnetic field is B z = B o sin(ωt + kx) {tex}\Rightarrow{B_z} = ({10^{ - 7}})\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]T{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}{E_y} = 30\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]V{m^{ - 1}}{/tex}

  1. On comparing the given equation with Ey = E₀ sin(ωt + kx), we get
    {tex}k = 300\pi = \frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \lambda = \frac{1}{{150}} = 6.7 \times {10^{ - 3}}m{/tex}
  2. The wave is propagating along X-axis, electric field is oscillating along Y-axis, so according to right hand system of (vector E, vector B, vector K), the magnetic field must oscillate along Z-axis.
    On comparing the given equation with E = E₀ sin(ωt + kx), we get,
    Eo = 30 V/m
    Now, Bo = {tex}\frac {E_o}c{/tex} = {tex}\frac{30}{3\times10^8}{/tex} = 10-7 T
    Thus, equation of oscillating magnetic field is
    Bz = Bo sin(ωt + kx)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{B_z} = ({10^{ - 7}})\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]T{/tex}

question_text: The magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by B y = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t). Calculate the Energy density associated with the Electromagnetic waves Speed of the wave.
explanation: Given: Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave B y = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t) Now B 0 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 T Speed of light c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s From the relation: E 0 = B 0 c = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 E 0 = 36 V/m Now Energy Density can be calculated using: Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times(36)^{2}{/tex} Energy Density of electromagnetic waves = 5.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 J/m 3 Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave B y = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t) Now k = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 {tex}\omega=3.6 \times 10^{15}{/tex} Since, {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{k}{/tex} and {tex}f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}{/tex} Hence the speed of wave: v = {tex}\lambda \times f{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\omega}{k}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
solution: Given: Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave B y = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t) Now B 0 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 T Speed of light c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s From the relation: E 0 = B 0 c = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 E 0 = 36 V/m Now Energy Density can be calculated using: Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times(36)^{2}{/tex} Energy Density of electromagnetic waves = 5.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -15 J/m 3 Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave B y = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 15 t) Now k = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 {tex}\omega=3.6 \times 10^{15}{/tex} Since, {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{k}{/tex} and {tex}f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}{/tex} Hence the speed of wave: v = {tex}\lambda \times f{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\omega}{k}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given:

  1. Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave By = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015t)
    Now B0 = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8T
    Speed of light c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex}108 m/s
    From the relation: E0 = B0c
    = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108
    E0 = 36 V/m
    Now Energy Density can be calculated using:
    Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves  = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
    Energy Density of the electromagnetic waves= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \times\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times(36)^{2}{/tex}
    Energy Density of electromagnetic waves = 5.74 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-15 J/m3
  2. Magnetic field in plane electromagnetic wave
    By = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 sin (1.20 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 z + 3.60 {tex}\times{/tex} 1015t)
    Now k = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 107
    {tex}\omega=3.6 \times 10^{15}{/tex}
    Since, {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{k}{/tex} and {tex}f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}{/tex}
    Hence the speed of wave: v = {tex}\lambda \times f{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\omega}{k}=3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}{/tex}

question_html:
1. Which one of the following electromagnetic radiations has least frequency:
    UV radiations, X-rays, Microwaves?
  2. How do you show that electromagnetic waves carry energy and momentum?
  3. Write the expression for the energy density of an electromagnetic wave propagating in free space.
question_text: Which one of the following electromagnetic radiations has least frequency: UV radiations, X-rays, Microwaves? How do you show that electromagnetic waves carry energy and momentum? Write the expression for the energy density of an electromagnetic wave propagating in free space.
explanation: Microwave When a charge oscillates with some frequency. It produces an oscillating electric field and magnetic field in space. So, an electromagnetic wave is produced. The frequency of the em wave is equal to the frequency of oscillation of the charge. Hence energy associated with the em wave comes at the expense of the energy of the source. If the em wave of energy U strikes on a surface and gets completely absorbed, total momentum delivery to the surface is p = {tex}\frac{U}{E}{/tex} . Hence em wave also carry momentum. The em wave consists of oscillating electric and magnetic fields, So net energy density of em wave is U = U E + U B U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon_0 E^2+\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^2}{\mu_0}{/tex}
solution: Microwave When a charge oscillates with some frequency. It produces an oscillating electric field and magnetic field in space. So, an electromagnetic wave is produced. The frequency of the em wave is equal to the frequency of oscillation of the charge. Hence energy associated with the em wave comes at the expense of the energy of the source. If the em wave of energy U strikes on a surface and gets completely absorbed, total momentum delivery to the surface is p = {tex}\frac{U}{E}{/tex} . Hence em wave also carry momentum. The em wave consists of oscillating electric and magnetic fields, So net energy density of em wave is U = U E + U B U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon_0 E^2+\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^2}{\mu_0}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Microwave
  2. When a charge oscillates with some frequency. It produces an oscillating electric field and magnetic field in space. So, an electromagnetic wave is produced. The frequency of the em wave is equal to the frequency of oscillation of the charge. Hence energy associated with the em wave comes at the expense of the energy of the source. If the em wave of energy U strikes on a surface and gets completely absorbed, total momentum delivery to the surface is p = {tex}\frac{U}{E}{/tex}.
    Hence em wave also carry momentum.
  3. The em wave consists of oscillating electric and magnetic fields, So net energy density of em wave is
    U = UE + UB
    U = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}{tex}\varepsilon_0 E^2+\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^2}{\mu_0}{/tex}

question_text: Given: Wavelength of light in mercury is {tex}5.5 \times {10^{ - 5}}cm{/tex} . Calculate its frequency and period. What is the wavelength of the light in the glass, if the refractive index of glass is 1.5?
explanation: Given: Wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5.5×10 −5 cm = 5.5×10 −7 m Frequency, {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}m/s}}{{5.5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}m}}{/tex} = 5.45×10 14 Hz {tex} = 5.45 \times {10^8}MHz{/tex} Time period, {tex}T = \frac{1}{\nu }{/tex} = {tex}\frac 1{ 5.45 \times {10^8}}{/tex} {tex}= 1.83 \times {10^{ - 9}}\mu s{/tex} Wavelength of the light in the glass, {tex}\lambda_g{/tex} = {tex}\frac {\lambda}{\mu}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.5\times10^{-7}\;}{1.5}{/tex} = 3.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m
solution: Given: Wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5.5×10 −5 cm = 5.5×10 −7 m Frequency, {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}m/s}}{{5.5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}m}}{/tex} = 5.45×10 14 Hz {tex} = 5.45 \times {10^8}MHz{/tex} Time period, {tex}T = \frac{1}{\nu }{/tex} = {tex}\frac 1{ 5.45 \times {10^8}}{/tex} {tex}= 1.83 \times {10^{ - 9}}\mu s{/tex} Wavelength of the light in the glass, {tex}\lambda_g{/tex} = {tex}\frac {\lambda}{\mu}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.5\times10^{-7}\;}{1.5}{/tex} = 3.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m
solution_html:
Given: Wavelength, {tex}\lambda{/tex} = 5.5×10−5 cm = 5.5×10−7 m

  1. Frequency, {tex}\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda } = \frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}m/s}}{{5.5 \times {{10}^{ - 7}}m}}{/tex} = 5.45×1014 Hz {tex} = 5.45 \times {10^8}MHz{/tex}
    Time period, {tex}T = \frac{1}{\nu }{/tex} = {tex}\frac 1{ 5.45 \times {10^8}}{/tex} {tex}= 1.83 \times {10^{ - 9}}\mu s{/tex}
  2. Wavelength of the light in the glass, {tex}\lambda_g{/tex} = {tex}\frac {\lambda}{\mu}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{5.5\times10^{-7}\;}{1.5}{/tex} = 3.67{tex}\times{/tex}10-7 m

question_text: A plane electromagnetic wave is moving along x-direction. The frequency of the wave is 10 15 Hz and the electric field at any point is varying sinu-soidally with time with an amplitude of 2 Vm -1 . Calculate the average densities of the electric and magnetic fields.
explanation: Here, v = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz, E 0 = 2Vm -1 Average energy density of the electric field {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{4} \in_{0} E_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{4} \times\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times 2^{2}{/tex} = 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 Jm -3 Average energy density of magnetic field {tex}u_{B}=\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{4 \mu_{0}}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{\left(E_{0} / c\right)^{2}}{\mu_{0}}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{E_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0} c^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{2^{2}}{\left(4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 Jm -3
solution: Here, v = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 14 Hz, E 0 = 2Vm -1 Average energy density of the electric field {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{4} \in_{0} E_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{4} \times\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times 2^{2}{/tex} = 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 Jm -3 Average energy density of magnetic field {tex}u_{B}=\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{4 \mu_{0}}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{\left(E_{0} / c\right)^{2}}{\mu_{0}}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{E_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0} c^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{2^{2}}{\left(4 \pi \times 10^{-7}\right) \times\left(3 \times 10^{8}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -12 Jm -3
solution_html:
Here, v = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1014 Hz, E0 = 2Vm-1

  1. Average energy density of the electric field
    {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{4} \in_{0} E_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{4} \times\left(8.85 \times 10^{-12}\right) \times 2^{2}{/tex}
    = 8.85 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-12Jm-3
  2. Average energy density of magnetic field
    {tex}u_{B}=\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{4 \mu_{0}}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{\left(E_{0} / c\right)^{2}}{\mu_{0}}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{E_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0} c^{2}}{/tex}

question_html: The charge on a parallel plate capacitor varies as q = q0 cos 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}t. The plates are very large and close together (area = A, separation = d). Neglecting the edge effects, find the displacement current through the capacitor?
question_text: The charge on a parallel plate capacitor varies as q = q 0 cos 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {tex}\nu{/tex} t. The plates are very large and close together (area = A, separation = d). Neglecting the edge effects, find the displacement current through the capacitor?
solution_html:
The displacement current in a capacitor is equal to the conduction current of the capacitor.
Displacement current, Id​ = Ic
The displacement current through the capacitor is given by
Id = Ic = {tex}\frac{d q}{d t}{/tex}........(i)
Here we are given, q = q0 cos 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}t
Putting this value in Eq (i), we get
Id = Ic = - q0 sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}t {tex}\times{/tex} 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}
Id = Ic = -2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}q0 sin 2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex}\nu{/tex}t

question_html: Sea water at frequency ν = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 Hz has permittivity {tex}\varepsilon \approx 80 \varepsilon_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}, permeability {tex}\mu \approx \mu_{o}{/tex} and resistivity ρ = 0.25 {tex}\Omega{/tex}-m. Imagine a parallel plate capacitor immersed in sea water and driven by an alternating voltage source V(t) = Vo sin (2{tex}\pi{/tex} νt). What fraction of the conduction current density is the displacement current density?
question_text: Sea water at frequency ν = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 Hz has permittivity {tex}\varepsilon \approx 80 \varepsilon_{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} , permeability {tex}\mu \approx \mu_{o}{/tex} and resistivity ρ = 0.25 {tex}\Omega{/tex} -m. Imagine a parallel plate capacitor immersed in sea water and driven by an alternating voltage source V(t) = V o sin (2 {tex}\pi{/tex} νt). What fraction of the conduction current density is the displacement current density?
solution_html:
Let the separation between the plates of capacitor immersed in sea water plates is d and applied voltage across the plates is V(t) = V0 sin (2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex} u{/tex}t)
Now using Ohm's law, the conduction current density {tex}J^{c}= rac{E}{ ho}= rac{1}{ ho} rac{V_{0}}{d}{/tex} sin (2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex} u{/tex}t)
{tex}\Rightarrow J^{c}= rac{V_{0}}{ ho d}{/tex} sin (2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex} u{/tex}t) {tex}=J_{0}^{c}{/tex} sin (2{tex}\pi{/tex}{tex} u{/tex}t)
Hence, {tex}J_{0}^{c}= rac{V_{0}}{ ho d}{/tex}
The displacement current density is given as
{tex}J^{d}=\varepsilon rac{d E}{d t}=\varepsilon rac{d}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\left[ rac{V_{0}}{d} \sin (2 \pi u t) ight]{/tex}
{tex}= rac{\varepsilon 2 \pi v V_{0}}{d} \cos (2 \pi u t){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow J^{d}=J_{0}^{d} \cos (2 \pi u t){/tex}
where, {tex}J_{0}^{d}= rac{2 \pi u \varepsilon V_{0}}{d}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad rac{J_{0}^{d}}{J_{0}^{c}}= rac{ rac{2 \pi u \varepsilon V_{0}}{d}}{ rac{V_{0}}{ ho d}}=2 \pi u\varepsilon ho{/tex}
{tex}=2 \pi imes 80 \varepsilon_{0} u imes 0.25{/tex} {tex}=4 \pi \varepsilon_{0} u imes 10{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow rac{J_{0}^{d}}{J_{0}^{c}}= rac{10 imes 4 imes 10^{8}}{9 imes 10^{9}}= rac{4}{9}{/tex} ,this is required result.

question_html: Light with an energy flux of 18 W/cm2 falls on a non-reflecting surface at normal incidence. If the surface has an area of 20 cm2, find the average force exerted on the surface during a 30-minute time span.
question_text: Light with an energy flux of 18 W/cm 2 falls on a non-reflecting surface at normal incidence. If the surface has an area of 20 cm 2 , find the average force exerted on the surface during a 30-minute time span.
explanation: t = 30 minute = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 1800 sec = 0.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 sec. The total energy falling on the surface is U = (18 W/cm 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} (20 cm 2 ) {tex}\times{/tex} (30 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 s) = 6.48 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 5 J Therefore, the total momentum delivered (for complete absorption) is {tex}p=\frac{U}{c}=\frac{6.48 \times 10^{5} \mathrm{J}}{3 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}}{/tex} = 2.16 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -3 kg m/s The average force exerted on the surface is {tex}F=\frac{p}{t}=\frac{2.16 \times 10^{-3}}{0.18 \times 10^{4}}{/tex} = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -6 N
solution: t = 30 minute = 30 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 1800 sec = 0.18 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 4 sec. question_html:
In a plane electromagnetic wave, the electric field oscillates sinusoidally at a frequency of 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1010 Hz and amplitude 48 V m-1.

  1. What is the wavelength of the wave?
  2. What is the amplitude of the oscillating magnetic field?
  3. Show that the average energy density of the E field equals the average energy density of the B field. [c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 ms-1]
question_text: In a plane electromagnetic wave, the electric field oscillates sinusoidally at a frequency of 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 10 Hz and amplitude 48 V m -1 . What is the wavelength of the wave? What is the amplitude of the oscillating magnetic field? Show that the average energy density of the E field equals the average energy density of the B field. [c = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 ms -1 ]
explanation: Given, {tex}\nu{/tex} = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 10 Hz, E 0 = 48 Vm -1 wavelength of the wave is {tex}\lambda = \frac{c}{\nu}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{2.0 \times {{10}^{10}}}}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m {tex}{B_0} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{48}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{/tex} = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 The average density of the electric field is given by {tex}{U_e} = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}{E^2}{/tex} and the average density of the magnetic field is given by {tex}{U_B} = \frac{{{B^2}}}{{2{\mu _0}}}{/tex} But B = {tex}\frac{E}{c}{/tex} and c = {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}} }}{/tex} , hence, the above equation becomes {tex}{U_B} = \frac{{{B^2}}}{{2{\mu _0}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{E^2}}}{{2{\mu _0} \times \frac{1}{{{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}{E^2}{/tex} = U e Hence, proved.
solution: Given, {tex}\nu{/tex} = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 10 Hz, E 0 = 48 Vm -1 wavelength of the wave is {tex}\lambda = \frac{c}{\nu}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{2.0 \times {{10}^{10}}}}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -2 m {tex}{B_0} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{48}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{/tex} = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 The average density of the electric field is given by {tex}{U_e} = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}{E^2}{/tex} and the average density of the magnetic field is given by {tex}{U_B} = \frac{{{B^2}}}{{2{\mu _0}}}{/tex} But B = {tex}\frac{E}{c}{/tex} and c = {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}} }}{/tex} , hence, the above equation becomes {tex}{U_B} = \frac{{{B^2}}}{{2{\mu _0}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{E^2}}}{{2{\mu _0} \times \frac{1}{{{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}{E^2}{/tex} = U e Hence, proved.
solution_html:
Given, {tex}\nu{/tex} = 2.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1010 Hz, E0 = 48 Vm-1

  1. wavelength of the wave is {tex}\lambda = \frac{c}{\nu}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{{2.0 \times {{10}^{10}}}}{/tex} = 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 m
  2. {tex}{B_0} = \frac{{{E_0}}}{c}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{48}}{{3 \times {{10}^8}}}{/tex}  = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7
  3. The average density of the electric field is given by
    {tex}{U_e} = \frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}{E^2}{/tex} 
    and the average density of the magnetic field is given by 
    {tex}{U_B} = \frac{{{B^2}}}{{2{\mu _0}}}{/tex}
    But B = {tex}\frac{E}{c}{/tex} and c = {tex}\frac{1}{{\sqrt {{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}} }}{/tex}, hence, the above equation becomes 
    {tex}{U_B} = \frac{{{B^2}}}{{2{\mu _0}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{{{E^2}}}{{2{\mu _0} \times \frac{1}{{{\mu _0}{\varepsilon _0}}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{\varepsilon _0}{E^2}{/tex} = Ue
    Hence, proved.

question_html:
The oscillating magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by {tex}{B_y} = (8 \times {10^{ - 6}})\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]T{/tex}

  1. Calculate the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave.
  2. Write down the expression for the oscillating electric field.
question_text: The oscillating magnetic field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by {tex}{B_y} = (8 \times {10^{ - 6}})\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]T{/tex} Calculate the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave. Write down the expression for the oscillating electric field.
explanation: Given equation is: {tex}{B_y} = (8 \times {10^{ - 6}})\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]T{/tex} Comparing the given equation with the equation of magnetic field varying sinusoidally with x and t, {tex}{B_y} = {B_0}\sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi x}}{\lambda } + \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}} \right){/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } = 300\pi {/tex} Thus, the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave is, {tex}\lambda = \frac{2}{{300}} = 0.0067m{/tex} {tex}{B_0} = 8 \times {10^{ - 6}}T{/tex} {tex}{E_0} = c{B_0} = 3 \times {10^8} \times 8 \times {10^{ - 6}}{/tex} {tex} = 24 \times {10^2} = 2400V{m^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The required expression for the oscillating electric field is, {tex}{E_z} = {E_0}\sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi x}}{\lambda } + \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}} \right){/tex} {tex}= 2400\sin (300\pi x + 2 \times {10^{11}}t)V/m{/tex}
solution: Given equation is: {tex}{B_y} = (8 \times {10^{ - 6}})\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]T{/tex} Comparing the given equation with the equation of magnetic field varying sinusoidally with x and t, {tex}{B_y} = {B_0}\sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi x}}{\lambda } + \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}} \right){/tex} , we get {tex}\frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } = 300\pi {/tex} Thus, the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave is, {tex}\lambda = \frac{2}{{300}} = 0.0067m{/tex} {tex}{B_0} = 8 \times {10^{ - 6}}T{/tex} {tex}{E_0} = c{B_0} = 3 \times {10^8} \times 8 \times {10^{ - 6}}{/tex} {tex} = 24 \times {10^2} = 2400V{m^{ - 1}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} The required expression for the oscillating electric field is, {tex}{E_z} = {E_0}\sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi x}}{\lambda } + \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}} \right){/tex} {tex}= 2400\sin (300\pi x + 2 \times {10^{11}}t)V/m{/tex}
solution_html:
Given equation is:
{tex}{B_y} = (8 \times {10^{ - 6}})\sin \left[ {2 \times {{10}^{11}}t + 300\pi x} \right]T{/tex}

  1. Comparing the given equation with the equation of magnetic field varying sinusoidally with x and t,
    {tex}{B_y} = {B_0}\sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi x}}{\lambda } + \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}} \right){/tex}, we get
    {tex}\frac{{2\pi }}{\lambda } = 300\pi {/tex}
    Thus, the wavelength of the electromagnetic wave is,
    {tex}\lambda = \frac{2}{{300}} = 0.0067m{/tex}
  2. {tex}{B_0} = 8 \times {10^{ - 6}}T{/tex}
    {tex}{E_0} = c{B_0} = 3 \times {10^8} \times 8 \times {10^{ - 6}}{/tex}
    {tex} = 24 \times {10^2} = 2400V{m^{ - 1}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} The required expression for the oscillating electric field is,
    {tex}{E_z} = {E_0}\sin \left( {\frac{{2\pi x}}{\lambda } + \frac{{2\pi t}}{T}} \right){/tex}
    {tex}= 2400\sin (300\pi x + 2 \times {10^{11}}t)V/m{/tex}

E)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_\alpha(\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E} .)_\alpha}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 m_p(K . \mathrm{E} \cdot)_p}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{qV}}{\mathrm{m}}} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m}_\alpha \mathrm{v}_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{h}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{m}_\alpha} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}} V}{\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{p}}}} \times \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_\alpha}{2 \mathrm{q}_\alpha V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{q_p}{q_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}

E)}{/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_\alpha(\mathrm{K} \cdot \mathrm{E} .)_\alpha}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\sqrt{2 m_p(K . \mathrm{E} \cdot)_p}}{h}{/tex} = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} v = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{qV}}{\mathrm{m}}} {/tex} {tex}\frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_{\mathrm{p}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{m}_\alpha \mathrm{v}_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}} \mathrm{v}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{h}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{p}}}{\mathrm{m}_\alpha} \sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{p}} V}{\mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{p}}}} \times \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{m}_\alpha}{2 \mathrm{q}_\alpha V}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{m_p}{m_\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\sqrt{\frac{q_p}{q_\alpha}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex}

question_text: A parallel plate capacitor made of circular plates each of radius R = 6.0 cm has a capacitance C = 100 pF. The capacitor is connected to a 230 V ac supply with an angular frequency of 300 rad s -1 . What is the rms value of the conduction current? Figure shows a capacitor made of two circular plates each of radius 12 cm, and separated by 5.0 cm. The capacitor is being charged by an external source. The charging current is constant and equal to 0.15 A.

  1. Calculate the capacitance and the rate of charge of the potential difference between the plates.
  2. Obtain the displacement current across the plates.
  3. Is Kirchoff's first rule (junction rule) valid at each plate of the capacitor? Explain.
question_text: Figure shows a capacitor made of two circular plates each of radius 12 cm, and separated by 5.0 cm. The capacitor is being charged by an external source. The charging current is constant and equal to 0.15 A. Calculate the capacitance and the rate of charge of the potential difference between the plates. Obtain the displacement current across the plates. Is Kirchoff's first rule (junction rule) valid at each plate of the capacitor? Explain.
explanation: Given, Radius R is = 12 cm = 0.12 m, separation between the plates is given by; d = 5.0 cm = {tex}5 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} m I = 0.15 A {tex}{\varepsilon _0} = 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}}{C^2}{N^{ - 1}}{m^{ - 2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Area, {tex}A = \pi {R^2} = 3.14 \times {(0.12)^2}{m^2}{/tex} Capacitance of parallel plate capacitor is given by {tex}C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{{8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}} \times (3.14) \times {{(0.12)}^2}}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 80.1 \times {10^{ - 12}} = 80.1pF{/tex} Now, q = CV or {tex}\frac{{dq}}{{dt}} = C \times \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}{/tex} or {tex}I = C \times \frac{{dV}}{{dt}}\left[ {\because I = \frac{{dq}}{{dt}}} \right]{/tex} or {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dt}} = \frac{I}{C} = \frac{{0.15}}{{80.1 \times {{10}^{ - 12}}}}{/tex} {tex} = 1.87 \times {10^9}V{s^{ - 1}}{/tex} Displacement current is equal to the conduction current i.e. 0.15 A. Yes, Kirchoff's first rule is valid at each plate of the capacitor provided. We take the current to be the sum of the conduction and displacement currents.

1. Given, Radius R is = 12 cm = 0.12 m, separation between the plates is given by;
    d = 5.0 cm = {tex}5 \times {10^{ - 2}}{/tex} m
    I = 0.15 A
    {tex}{\varepsilon _0} = 8.85 \times {10^{ - 12}}{C^2}{N^{ - 1}}{m^{ - 2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Area, {tex}A = \pi {R^2} = 3.14 \times {(0.12)^2}{m^2}{/tex}
    Capacitance of parallel plate capacitor is given by
    {tex}C = \frac{{{\varepsilon _0}A}}{d}{/tex}
    {tex} = \frac{{8.85 \times {{10}^{ - 12}} \times (3.14) \times {{(0.12)}^2}}}{{5 \times {{10}^{ - 3}}}}{/tex}

  2. Displacement current is equal to the conduction current i.e. 0.15 A.

question_html:
When a parallel plate capacitor is being charged by a dc battery, why does a galvanometer show a momentary deflection?
Obtain the expression for the displacement current and show how it is related to conduction current in a steady state.
question_text: When a parallel plate capacitor is being charged by a dc battery, why does a galvanometer show a momentary deflection? Obtain the expression for the displacement current and show how it is related to conduction current in a steady state.
explanation: While being charged, electron flow from battery into the capacitor until the capacitors is completely charged. As a result, galvanometer shows deflection. Between the plates of the capacitor {tex}\emptyset_E=|E| A=\frac{Q}{A \epsilon_O} A=\frac{Q}{\epsilon_O}{/tex} Q changes with time and current {tex}i=\frac{d \emptyset}{d t}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{d \emptyset_E}{d t}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_O} \frac{d \emptyset}{d t} \Rightarrow i=\epsilon_O \frac{d Q}{d t}{/tex} This is the displacement current i d . Outside the plates, the current in the conductor due to the flow of charges is called conduction current.
solution: While being charged, electron flow from battery into the capacitor until the capacitors is completely charged. As a result, galvanometer shows deflection. Between the plates of the capacitor {tex}\emptyset_E=|E| A=\frac{Q}{A \epsilon_O} A=\frac{Q}{\epsilon_O}{/tex} Q changes with time and current {tex}i=\frac{d \emptyset}{d t}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{d \emptyset_E}{d t}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_O} \frac{d \emptyset}{d t} \Rightarrow i=\epsilon_O \frac{d Q}{d t}{/tex} This is the displacement current i d . Outside the plates, the current in the conductor due to the flow of charges is called conduction current.

While being charged, electron flow from battery into the capacitor until the capacitors is completely charged.
As a result, galvanometer shows deflection.
Between the plates of the capacitor
{tex}\emptyset_E=|E| A=\frac{Q}{A \epsilon_O} A=\frac{Q}{\epsilon_O}{/tex}
Q changes with time and current {tex}i=\frac{d \emptyset}{d t}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{d \emptyset_E}{d t}=\frac{1}{\epsilon_O} \frac{d \emptyset}{d t} \Rightarrow i=\epsilon_O \frac{d Q}{d t}{/tex}
This is the displacement current id.
Outside the plates, the current in the conductor due to the flow of charges is called conduction current.

question_text: Electromagnetic waves with wavelength {tex}\lambda_{1}{/tex} is used in satellite communication. {tex}\lambda_{2}{/tex} is used to kill germs in water purifies. {tex}\lambda_{3}{/tex} is used to detect leakage of oil in underground pipelines. {tex}\lambda_{4}{/tex} is used to improve visibility in runways during fog and mist conditions. question_html:
1. Identify the part of the electromagnetic spectrum which is
    1. suitable for radar system used in aircraft navigation,
    2. produced by bombarding a metal target by high speed electrons.
  2. Why does galvanometer show a momentary deflection at time of charging and discharging a capacitor? Write the necessary expression to explain this observation.
question_text: Identify the part of the electromagnetic spectrum which is suitable for radar system used in aircraft navigation, produced by bombarding a metal target by high speed electrons. Why does galvanometer show a momentary deflection at time of charging and discharging a capacitor? Write the necessary expression to explain this observation.
explanation: Microwaves are suitable for radar system used in aircraft navigation because of its shorter wavelength and frequency range from 1 GHz to 300 GHz. X-rays are produced by bombarding a metal target by high-speed electrons inside a Coolidge tube or X-ray tube by applying huge potential difference between the anode (metal target) and the cathode (electron gun). During charging and discharging of a capacitor, a conduction current is produced due to the flow of charges from battery to the plates and vice-versa via connecting wires. Due to this, galvanometer shows a momentary deflection. Now, when the capacitor is fully charged, the conduction current stops flowing. Also during charging and discharging, there is zero conduction current between the plates. But it was found that an electric field exist in between the plates, therefore indicating existence of magnetic field which leads to inconsistency in the Ampere's circuital law. To account this problem, the concept of displacement current was introduced by Maxwell. He changed the Ampere's circuital law, {tex}\left( \oint \mathbf { B } , d l = \mu _ { 0 } I \right){/tex} , to a modified form which is given as {tex}\oint \mathbf { B } d l = \mu _ { 0 }( I + I _ { D }){/tex} , where, I D is the displacement current produced due to change of electrostatic flux with time in between the two plates of the capacitor and is expressed as I D = {tex}\epsilon _0 \frac {d \phi _E}{dt}{/tex} .
solution: Microwaves are suitable for radar system used in aircraft navigation because of its shorter wavelength and frequency range from 1 GHz to 300 GHz. X-rays are produced by bombarding a metal target by high-speed electrons inside a Coolidge tube or X-ray tube by applying huge potential difference between the anode (metal target) and the cathode (electron gun). During charging and discharging of a capacitor, a conduction current is produced due to the flow of charges from battery to the plates and vice-versa via connecting wires. Due to this, galvanometer shows a momentary deflection. Now, when the capacitor is fully charged, the conduction current stops flowing. But it was found that an electric field exist in between the plates, therefore indicating existence of magnetic field which leads to inconsistency in the Ampere's circuital law. To account this problem, the concept of displacement current was introduced by Maxwell. He changed the Ampere's circuital law, {tex}\left( \oint \mathbf { B } , d l = \mu _ { 0 } I \right){/tex} , to a modified form which is given as {tex}\oint \mathbf { B } d l = \mu _ { 0 }( I + I _ { D }){/tex} , where, I D is the displacement current produced due to change of electrostatic flux with time in between the two plates of the capacitor and is expressed as I D = {tex}\epsilon _0 \frac {d \phi _E}{dt}{/tex} .

1. Microwaves are suitable for radar system used in aircraft navigation because of its shorter wavelength and frequency range from 1 GHz to 300 GHz.
    2. X-rays are produced by bombarding a metal target by high-speed electrons inside a Coolidge tube or X-ray tube by applying huge potential difference between the anode (metal target) and the cathode (electron gun).
  2. During charging and discharging of a capacitor, a conduction current is produced due to the flow of charges from battery to the plates and vice-versa via connecting wires. Due to this, galvanometer shows a momentary deflection. Now, when the capacitor is fully charged, the conduction current stops flowing. Also during charging and discharging, there is zero conduction current between the plates.
    But it was found that an electric field exist in between the plates, therefore indicating existence of magnetic field which leads to inconsistency in the Ampere's circuital law. To account this problem, the concept of displacement current was introduced by Maxwell. He changed the Ampere's circuital law, {tex}\left( \oint \mathbf { B } , d l = \mu _ { 0 } I \right){/tex}, to a modified form which is given as {tex}\oint \mathbf { B } d l = \mu _ { 0 }( I + I _ { D }){/tex} , where, ID is the displacement current produced due to change of electrostatic flux with time in between the two plates of the capacitor and is expressed as ID = {tex}\epsilon _0 \frac {d \phi _E}{dt}{/tex}.

question_html: How are electromagnetic waves produced by oscillating charges? Draw a sketch of linearly polarised electromagnetic waves propagating in the Z-direction. Indicate the directions of the oscillating electric and magnetic fields.
question_text: How are electromagnetic waves produced by oscillating charges? Draw a sketch of linearly polarised electromagnetic waves propagating in the Z-direction. Indicate the directions of the oscillating electric and magnetic fields.
explanation: The oscillating charge produces an oscillating (i.e. time-varying) electric field. The oscillating electric field produces a magnetic field which is also time-varying. This time-varying magnetic field produces an oscillating emf or voltage. An oscillating voltage (emf) produces an oscillating magnetic field and so on. This, in turn, produces an oscillating electric field and so on. Thus oscillating electric and magnetic fields regenerate each other producing electromagnetic wave propagating through space. In this way, the oscillating charge produces an electromagnetic wave. An oscillating L-C circuit can produce electromagnetic waves of frequency as charge oscillates across the capacitor's plates in this circuit. The frequency of oscillation is given by {tex}\nu=\frac1{2\pi} \sqrt {LC}{/tex} . This frequency is equal to the frequency of EM waves. The sketch of linearly polarised EM wave propagating in Z-direction is shown in the diagram below. The direction of propagation of an electromagnetic wave is determined from the cross product of electric and magnetic field i.e. {tex}\vec E × \vec B{/tex} . Hence the electric field intensity, {tex}\vec E{/tex} will be along X-axis and magnetic field intensity, {tex}\vec B{/tex} will be along Y-axis because the two fields are perpendicular to each other and also perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the electromagnetic wave. i.e. perpendicular to the Z-axis.

question_html:
Answer the following questions:

  1. Show, by giving a simple example, how EM waves carry energy and momentum.
  2. How are microwaves produced? Why is it necessary in microwaves ovens to select the frequency of microwaves to match the resonant frequency of water molecules?
  3. Write two important uses of infrared waves.
question_text: Answer the following questions: Show, by giving a simple example, how EM waves carry energy and momentum. How are microwaves produced? Why is it necessary in microwaves ovens to select the frequency of microwaves to match the resonant frequency of water molecules? Write two important uses of infrared waves.
explanation: Consider a plane perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave. An electric charge, on the plane, will be set in motion by the electric and magnetic fields of EM wave, incident on this plane. This is only possible if the EM wave constitutes momentum (the momentum value of an EM wave can be obtained using De Broglie's formula) and energy. Thus, this illustrates that EM waves carry energy and momentum. Microwaves are produced by a special vacuum tube like the klystron, magnetron and Gunn diode. The frequency of microwaves is selected to match the resonant frequency of water molecules causing more and more vibrations to the water molecules. Thus, the vibrations cause the increase in velocity, so that energy is transformed efficiently to increase the kinetic energy of the molecules. Hence, a microwave oven can cook food with water molecules inside. Uses of Infrared Rays: In knowing the atomic and molecular structure of solid substances and therapy to heal muscular pain. In remote control of TV, VCR, etc.

A plane EM wave travelling along z direction is described by {tex}\vec E{/tex} = E0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) {tex}\hat i{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} = B0 (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t) {tex}\hat j{/tex}. Show that.

  1. The average energy density of the wave is given by {tex}u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{\mathrm{B}_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
  2. The time averaged intensity of the wave is given by {tex}I_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{c} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
question_text: A plane EM wave travelling along z direction is described by {tex}\vec E{/tex} = E 0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) {tex}\hat i{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex} = B 0 (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) {tex}\hat j{/tex} . Show that. The average energy density of the wave is given by {tex}u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{\mathrm{B}_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} The time averaged intensity of the wave is given by {tex}I_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{c} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
explanation: Total energy carried by electromagnetic wave is due to electric field vector and magnetic field vector. In electromagnetic wave, E and B vary from point to point and from moment to moment. The energy density due to electric field E is given by:- {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} The energy density due to magnetic field B is given by: {tex}u_{B}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} The total energy density of the electromagnetic wave is given by: u = u E + u B {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} Given that The electric field vector and magnetic field vector be represented by E = E 0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) B = B 0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) The values of E 2 and B 2 vary from point to point and from moment to moment. Hence, the effective values of E 2 and B 2 are their time averages over complete cycle. We know, {tex}\left\langle\sin ^{2} \theta\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin ^{2} \theta d \theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\left\langle\cos ^{2} \theta\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \cos ^{2} \theta d \theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, the time average value of E 2 over complete cycle is given by, {tex}\left\langle B^{2}\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{T}\left[B_{0} \sin (k z-\omega t)\right]^{2} d t}{T}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{2}{/tex} The time average of energy density over complete cycle is given by- {tex}u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \mu_{0}\left(\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} We know {tex}c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}=\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}{/tex} where {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Absolute permeability, {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = Absolute permittivity, E 0 and B 0 = Amplitudes of electric field and magnetic field vectors The time average of energy density due to magnetic field B is {tex}u_{B}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{\left(E_{0}^{2} / c^{2}\right)}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{E_{0}^{2}}{4 \mu_{0}} \times \mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} Hence, u B = u E ; the time average of energy density due to magnetic field. {tex}\Rightarrow u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} Time average intensity of the wave is given by: I av = u av c {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}c=\frac{1}{2} c \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
solution: Total energy carried by electromagnetic wave is due to electric field vector and magnetic field vector. In electromagnetic wave, E and B vary from point to point and from moment to moment. The energy density due to electric field E is given by:- {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} The energy density due to magnetic field B is given by: {tex}u_{B}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} The total energy density of the electromagnetic wave is given by: u = u E + u B {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} Given that The electric field vector and magnetic field vector be represented by E = E 0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) B = B 0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex} t) The values of E 2 and B 2 vary from point to point and from moment to moment. Hence, the effective values of E 2 and B 2 are their time averages over complete cycle. We know, {tex}\left\langle\sin ^{2} \theta\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin ^{2} \theta d \theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and {tex}\left\langle\cos ^{2} \theta\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \cos ^{2} \theta d \theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Hence, the time average value of E 2 over complete cycle is given by, {tex}\left\langle B^{2}\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{T}\left[B_{0} \sin (k z-\omega t)\right]^{2} d t}{T}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{2}{/tex} The time average of energy density over complete cycle is given by- {tex}u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \mu_{0}\left(\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} We know {tex}c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}=\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}{/tex} where {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Absolute permeability, {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = Absolute permittivity, E 0 and B 0 = Amplitudes of electric field and magnetic field vectors The time average of energy density due to magnetic field B is {tex}u_{B}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{\left(E_{0}^{2} / c^{2}\right)}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{E_{0}^{2}}{4 \mu_{0}} \times \mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} Hence, u B = u E ; the time average of energy density due to magnetic field. {tex}\Rightarrow u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} Time average intensity of the wave is given by: I av = u av c {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}c=\frac{1}{2} c \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Total energy carried by electromagnetic wave is due to electric field vector and magnetic field vector. In electromagnetic wave, E and B vary from point to point and from moment to moment.
    The energy density due to electric field E is given by:-
    {tex}u_{E}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex}
    The energy density due to magnetic field B is given by:
    {tex}u_{B}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    The total energy density of the electromagnetic wave is given by:
    u =  uE + uB {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{B^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    Given that The electric field vector and magnetic field vector be represented by
    E = E0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
    B = B0 sin (kz - {tex}\omega{/tex}t)
    The values of E2 and B2 vary from point to point and from moment to moment. Hence, the effective values of E2 and B2 are their time averages over complete cycle.
    We know, {tex}\left\langle\sin ^{2} \theta\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \sin ^{2} \theta d \theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    and {tex}\left\langle\cos ^{2} \theta\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{2 \pi} \cos ^{2} \theta d \theta}{2 \pi}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    Hence, the time average value of E2 over complete cycle is given by,
    {tex}\left\langle B^{2}\right\rangle=\frac{\int_{0}^{T}\left[B_{0} \sin (k z-\omega t)\right]^{2} d t}{T}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{2}{/tex}
    The time average of energy density over complete cycle is given by-
    {tex}u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}}{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{2} \mu_{0}\left(\frac{B_{0}^{2}}{2}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
  2. We know {tex}c=\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}=\frac{E_{0}}{B_{0}}{/tex}
    where {tex}\mu_{0}{/tex} = Absolute permeability,  {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = Absolute permittivity, E0 and B0 = Amplitudes of electric field and magnetic field vectors
    The time average of energy density due to magnetic field B is
    {tex}u_{B}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{\left(E_{0}^{2} / c^{2}\right)}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{E_{0}^{2}}{4 \mu_{0}} \times \mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
    Hence, uB = uE; the time average of energy density due to magnetic field.
    {tex}\Rightarrow u_{\mathrm{av}}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex} {tex}+\frac{1}{4} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}+\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{B_{0}^{2}}{\mu_{0}}{/tex}
    Time average intensity of the wave is given by:
    Iav = uavc {tex}=\left(\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}c=\frac{1}{2} c \varepsilon_{0} E_{0}^{2}{/tex}

question_html:
Given below are some famous numbers associated with electromagnetic radiations in different contexts in physics. State the part of the electromagnetic spectrum to which each belongs.

  1. 21 cm (wavelength emitted by atomic hydrogen in interstellar space).
  2. 1057 MHz (frequency of radiation arising from two close energy levels in hydrogen; known as Lamb shift).
  3. 2.7 K [temperature associated with the isotropic radiation filling all space-thought to be a relic of the big-bang origin of the universe].
  4. 5890 {tex}\mathop A \limits^{o}{/tex} - 5896 {tex}\mathop A \limits^{o}{/tex} [double lines of sodium]
  5. 14.4 keV [energy of a particular transition in 57Fe nucleus associated with a famous high resolution spectroscopic method (Mössbauer spectroscopy)].
question_text: Given below are some famous numbers associated with electromagnetic radiations in different contexts in physics. State the part of the electromagnetic spectrum to which each belongs. 21 cm (wavelength emitted by atomic hydrogen in interstellar space). 1057 MHz (frequency of radiation arising from two close energy levels in hydrogen; known as Lamb shift). 2.7 K [temperature associated with the isotropic radiation filling all space-thought to be a relic of the big-bang origin of the universe]. 5890 {tex}\mathop A \limits^{o}{/tex} - 5896 {tex}\mathop A \limits^{o}{/tex} [double lines of sodium] 14.4 keV [energy of a particular transition in 57 Fe nucleus associated with a famous high resolution spectroscopic method (Mössbauer spectroscopy)].
solution_html:
1. Radio waves; it belongs to the short wavelength end of the electromagnetic spectrum.
  2. The frequency of radiation arising from the two close energy levels in hydrogen known as Lamb shift i.e. 1057 MHz is, radio waves; it belongs to the short wavelength end.
  3. Temperature, T = 2.7° K
    {tex}\lambda{/tex}m is given by Planck's law as:
    {tex}\lambda_{m}=\frac{0.29}{2.7}{/tex} = 0.11 cm
    This wavelength corresponds to microwaves.
  4. This is the yellow light of the visible spectrum.
  5. Transition energy is given by the relation,
    E = h{tex}\nu{/tex}
    Where,
    h = Planck's constant = 6.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-34 Js
    {tex}\nu{/tex} = Frequency of radiation
    Energy, E = 14.4 K eV
    {tex}\nu=\frac{E}{h}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{14.4 \times 10^{3} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{6.6 \times 10^{34}}{/tex}
    = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1018 Hz
    This corresponds to X-rays.

Maxwell showed that the speed of an electromagnetic wave depends on the permeability and permittivity of the medium through which it travels. The speed of an electromagnetic wave in free space is given by c = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_{0} \varepsilon_{0}}}{/tex}. The fact led Maxwell to predict that light is an electromagnetic wave. The emergence of the speed of light from purely electromagnetic considerations is the crowning achievement of Maxwell’s electromagnetic theory. The speed of an electromagnetic wave in any medium of permeability {tex}\mu{/tex} and permittivity {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} will be {tex}\frac{c}{\sqrt{K \mu_{r}}}{/tex} where K is the dielectric constant of the medium and {tex}\mu_{r}{/tex} is the relative permeability.

  1. What are the dimensions of {tex}\frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_{0} E^{2}{/tex} ({tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} : permittivity of free space; E = electric field).

  2. Find the dimensional formula of the permittivity of the vacuum.

  3. What would happen if an electromagnetic wave of frequency 3 MHz passes from vacuum into a dielectric medium with permittivity {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 4{tex}\epsilon _0{/tex}.

  4. Which of the following are not electromagnetic waves?
    cosmic rays, {tex}\gamma{/tex}-rays, {tex}\beta{/tex}-rays, X-rays

  5. Find the speed of electromagnetic waves.

    {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{AT}]^{2}}{\mathrm{MLT}^{-2} \mathrm{~L}^{2}}{/tex} = M-1L-3T4A2

  3. wavelength is halved and the frequency remains unchanged.
    The frequency of the electromagnetic wave remains same when it passes from one medium to another.
    Refractive index of the medium, n = {tex}\sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_{0}}} = \sqrt{\frac{4}{1}}{/tex} = 2
    Wavelength of the electromagnetic wave in the medium,
    {tex}\lambda_{\mathrm{med}}=\frac{\lambda}{n}=\frac{\lambda}{2}{/tex}

  4. {tex}\beta{/tex}-rays consists of electrons which are not electromagnetic in nature.

  5. The velocity of electromagnetic waves in free space (vacuum) is equal to velocity of light in vacuum (i.e., 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 m s-1).

Suppose that the electric field part of an electromagnetic wave in vacuum is E = (3.1 N/C) cos [(1.8 rad/m) y + (5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 rad/s)t] {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex}.

  1. What is the direction of propagation?
  2. What is the wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} ?

question_text: Suppose that the electric field part of an electromagnetic wave in vacuum is E = (3.1 N/C) cos [(1.8 rad/m) y + (5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 rad/s)t] {tex}\hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex} . What is the direction of propagation? What is the wavelength {tex}\lambda{/tex} ? What is the frequency {tex}\nu{/tex} ? What is the amplitude of the magnetic field part of the wave? Write an expression for the magnetic field part of the wave.
explanation: From the given electric field vector, it can be inferred that the electric field is directed along the negative x direction. Hence, the direction of motion is along the negative y direction i.e., -j. It is given that, {tex}\vec{E}{/tex} = 3.1 N/C cos [(1.8 rad/m) y + (5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 rad/s)t] {tex}\hat{\mathbf{{i}}}{/tex} ..(i) The general equation for the electric field vector in the positive x direction can be written as: {tex}\vec{E}=E_{0} \sin (k x-\omega t){/tex} ...(ii) On comparing equations (i) and (ii), we get Electric field amplitude,is E 0 = 3.1 N/C and the Angular frequency, {tex}\omega{/tex} = 5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 rad/s Wave number, k = 1.8 rad/m hence Wavelength is given by , {tex}\lambda=\frac{2 \pi}{1.8}{/tex} = 3.490 m Frequency of wave is given by: {tex}v=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{5.4 \times 10^{8}}{2 \pi}{/tex} = 8.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 Hz Magnetic field strength is given as: {tex}B_{0}=\frac{E_{0}}{c}{/tex} Where, c = Speed of light = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 8 m/s hence, amplitude of the magnetic field part of the wave is given by :- {tex}\therefore B_{0}=\frac{3.1}{3 \times 10^{8}}{/tex} = 1.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T On observing the given vector field, it can be observed that the magnetic field vector is directed along the negative z direction. Hence, the general equation for the magnetic field vector is written as: {tex}\vec{B}=B_{0} \cos (k y+\omega t)k{/tex} = (1.03 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 T) cos [(1.8 rad/m) y + (5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 6 rad/s)t] {tex}\hat{\mathbf{k}}{/tex}
